text:
A law firm owner called me last month, frustrated.

His agency had just sent a glowing monthly report — impressions up, clicks up, "great performance."

I asked one question: how many clients did it generate?

Silence.

That silence is costing firms millions.

What metrics does your agency report on? 👇

I audited a firm spending $9,000/month on Google Ads.

They thought it was working because the phone was ringing.

When we traced every lead to outcome:
— 61% were wrong practice area
— 22% never got a callback
— Only 8 retained clients in 90 days

$9K/month for 8 clients. The math didn't work.

We fixed the targeting, rebuilt intake, cut spend to $5K.

31 retained clients the next quarter.

Unpopular opinion:

Most law firm websites aren't a design problem.

They're an empathy problem.

Someone searching "Tampa DUI attorney" at 11pm is scared. They made a mistake. They don't know what happens next.

Your homepage should make that person feel like they found the right place.

Not impress other lawyers at a bar association meeting.

The most underused asset in legal marketing:

Your Google Business Profile.

It's free. It ranks. It drives calls.

Most firms set it up once in 2019 and forgot about it.

What a fully optimized GBP looks like:
✓ Every category filled
✓ 50+ reviews with responses
✓ Weekly posts
✓ Photos updated quarterly
✓ Q&A section populated

This alone outperforms $2K/month in ads for local intent searches.

The call came in at 9am on a Tuesday.

Partner at a 6-attorney firm. Frustrated. Their SEO agency had "ranked them #1" for their main keyword.

Problem: zero increase in retained clients over 12 months.

When I looked at the keyword — it was a term other lawyers search, not potential clients.

Ranking #1 for the wrong thing is worse than not ranking at all. It gives you false confidence.

Three questions every law firm should be able to answer:

1. What is your cost per retained client by practice area?
2. What percentage of leads become consultations?
3. What percentage of consultations become retained clients?

If you can answer all three, your marketing is working.

If you can't — you're flying blind with a running engine.

Which one do you struggle with most?

Referrals are wonderful.

They close faster, pay better, and complain less.

But here's what nobody says out loud:

You can't predict them. You can't scale them. You can't build a growth plan around them.

The firms hitting $3M+ have referrals AND a digital system that runs while they sleep.

One feeds the other. Neither replaces the other.

I've sat in a lot of law firm marketing meetings.

The pattern I see in firms that are struggling:

They talk about what they're spending.

The pattern in firms that are growing:

They talk about what each dollar returned.

Same budget. Completely different conversation.

The shift from input to output thinking changes everything.

Hot take that most agencies don't want you to hear:

Your intake process is worth more than your ad budget.

I've seen firms with $500/month in ads outperform competitors spending $8K — because they answered the phone fast, followed up twice, and made the consultation easy to book.

Fix the leak before you pour more water in.

AI search is changing how people find attorneys.

ChatGPT. Perplexity. Google's AI Overview.

People are asking: "Who is the best estate planning attorney in St. Petersburg FL?"

And they're getting a direct answer — not a list of links.

The firms that show up in those answers wrote content that answered real questions, consistently, for years.

The time to start was 2 years ago. The second best time is now.

Something I noticed after auditing 100+ law firm marketing setups:

The firms spending the most aren't usually winning.

The firms documenting the most are.

They know their numbers. They test one thing at a time. They kill what doesn't work fast.

Marketing discipline beats marketing budget every time.

What's one thing you've tested in your marketing this year?

A family law attorney in Tampa told me something that stuck with me:

"Our best clients find us at 2am when they can't sleep."

That's not a marketing insight. That's a human insight.

Your website needs to work at 2am. Your Google Business Profile needs to show up at 2am. Your intake form needs to be easy at 2am.

The clients who need you most aren't searching during business hours.

The law firms I've seen grow fastest in the last 3 years have one thing in common:

They publish consistently.

Not viral content. Not groundbreaking takes.

Just: one useful piece of content per week, every week, for 18 months.

Google rewards consistency more than brilliance.

So does trust.

Genuine question for law firm owners:

When did you last read a piece of content your agency wrote for you?

And when you read it — did it sound like you?

Content that doesn't sound like the attorney it's attributed to is a liability, not an asset.

Clients notice. Google notices.

Voice matters more than volume.

The worst thing that can happen to a law firm's marketing:

Not a bad campaign. Not a Google update.

A great campaign with a broken intake.

I've watched firms generate 200 leads in a month and retain 4 clients — because the follow-up system was held together with sticky notes and good intentions.

The pipeline and the intake have to be built together.

What I actually do when a new client brings me in:

Week 1: I don't touch their ads. I trace every lead from the last 90 days to outcome.

What I usually find:
— 30-40% of leads never got a response
— The best-performing channel is rarely what they think
— There's one practice area subsidizing the rest

The data tells you exactly where to focus. Most firms never look at it.

Law firm SEO myth:

"We rank #1, so SEO is working."

Ranking is a leading indicator. Retained clients are the only metric that matters.

I've worked with firms ranking #1 for years who were barely profitable from search — because the traffic was wrong, the page didn't convert, or intake dropped the ball.

Rank is the beginning of the story. Not the end.

Something nobody told me when I started in legal marketing:

The practice area matters more than the strategy.

Personal injury marketing is completely different from estate planning marketing.

Family law is different from business litigation.

The buyer's emotional state, search behavior, and decision timeline are different in every case.

One-size-fits-all agencies don't work in this industry. They never did.

I asked a managing partner last year: what does success look like for your marketing?

He said: "More calls."

I asked: from who?

Long pause.

That pause is where most law firm marketing strategies break down.

More calls from the wrong people costs you more than fewer calls from the right people.

Target first. Scale second.

20 years of legal marketing. The lesson that took longest to learn:

Clients don't hire the best attorney.

They hire the attorney who made them feel the most confident — at the moment they were most afraid.

Marketing's job isn't to shout the loudest.

It's to be the clearest, most credible voice in the room when someone needs help.

That's it. That's the whole strategy.
